title: Doctor Visit
description: Practice conversations at the doctor's office and hospital in English

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Good morning! I'm Dr. Chen. Please have a seat. What brings you in today?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Good morning, Doctor. I've been having some persistent headaches for about two weeks now and I'm a bit concerned.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: I see. Can you describe the headaches? Are they dull or sharp? Do they happen at a specific time of day?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: They're mostly a dull, throbbing pain, usually starting in the afternoon. They last a few hours and sometimes come with a bit of neck stiffness.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Are you experiencing any other symptoms — nausea, sensitivity to light or sound, blurred vision?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Occasionally some sensitivity to light, yes. No nausea though.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: On a scale of one to ten, how severe is the pain when it's at its worst?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I'd say about a six or seven. It makes it hard to concentrate at work.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Have you taken anything for the pain?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I've been taking over-the-counter ibuprofen, which helps a little but doesn't fully resolve it.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Any recent changes in your lifestyle — new stresses at work, less sleep, changes in your diet or fluid intake?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Actually, yes. I've been under a lot of stress at work lately and I think I haven't been drinking enough water. I've also been staring at my computer screen much longer than usual.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Those are likely contributors. Let me do a brief examination. Can you follow my finger with your eyes please?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Of course.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Good. I'm also going to check your blood pressure. Have you had it checked recently?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Not in about a year.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Your blood pressure is slightly elevated — 138 over 88. It's not critically high, but it's something to monitor. Given your symptoms and the timeline, I believe these are tension headaches, likely triggered by stress, dehydration, and eye strain.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: That's a relief. What would you recommend?

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: First, increase your water intake to at least two liters a day. Take regular breaks from your screen using the 20-20-20 rule — every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Try to manage stress with light exercise or meditation. If the headaches continue after two weeks, come back and we'll consider further tests.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Should I be concerned about the blood pressure?

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: It's borderline. I'd like you to reduce sodium in your diet and avoid caffeine after noon. Monitor it at a pharmacy over the next two weeks and bring me the readings.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I can do that. Do I need a prescription for anything?

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: Not at this stage. Continue ibuprofen as needed but don't exceed 1200mg per day. If headaches worsen or you develop fever, vision changes, or severe neck pain, go to the emergency room immediately.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Understood. Thank you so much, Doctor. I feel much better knowing what's happening.

lines.speaker: Doctor
lines.text: You're welcome. My receptionist will schedule a follow-up in two weeks. Take care of yourself.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: Hello, I'm Dr. Rivera, an allergist. I've reviewed your referral. Tell me more about your symptoms.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Hi, Doctor. For the past few months I've had constant sneezing, itchy eyes, and a runny nose. It seems worse in the mornings and when I'm near certain plants.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: Do you have any pets?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Yes, I have a cat. I've had her for two years though, and the symptoms only started recently.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: Allergies can develop at any age, even to animals you've owned for years. Have you noticed any skin reactions — hives, rashes, or itching?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Sometimes my arms get itchy after the cat sits on me.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: That's significant. Any asthma-like symptoms — wheezing or shortness of breath?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Occasionally, when I'm around heavy perfume or cigarette smoke.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: I'd like to run an allergy panel — both a skin prick test and a blood test — to identify specific triggers. Are you currently taking any antihistamines?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I've been taking loratadine daily. It helps somewhat but not completely.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: We'll need to pause antihistamines 72 hours before the skin test for accurate results. I'll also recommend you keep an allergy diary — note your symptoms, location, time of day, and any triggers you suspect.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: What if the tests confirm a cat allergy? I really don't want to give up my cat.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: That's understandable. If it's confirmed, we have options. Allergen immunotherapy — commonly called allergy shots — can desensitize your immune system over time. We can also prescribe stronger nasal corticosteroids and discuss environmental controls like air purifiers and keeping the cat out of your bedroom.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: How long does immunotherapy take?

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: Typically three to five years for full desensitization. But most patients see noticeable improvement within six months.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: That's reassuring. What should I do in the meantime?

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: Wash your hands after touching the cat, vacuum frequently with a HEPA filter, and try to keep windows closed during high pollen season. I'll prescribe a stronger antihistamine and a nasal spray to manage your symptoms while we wait for test results.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Thank you, Doctor. I'll schedule the allergy testing.

lines.speaker: Specialist
lines.text: Perfect. We'll have results in about a week and then we can build a proper management plan together.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Hi, I'm here to pick up a prescription. My doctor called it in earlier today.

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: Of course. What's the name on the prescription?

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Sarah Mitchell. Date of birth, March 15, 1990.

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: Let me check. Yes, I have it here — amoxicillin 500mg capsules. Is this your first time taking this medication?

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Yes, it is. My doctor prescribed it for a sinus infection.

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: I'll go over the important details with you. You'll take one capsule three times a day with food for ten days. It's very important to complete the full course, even if you start feeling better after a few days.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: What happens if I stop early?

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: Stopping antibiotics early can allow the bacteria to survive and become resistant. That could make future infections harder to treat.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Understood. Are there any side effects I should watch for?

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: Common side effects include mild stomach upset, diarrhea, and sometimes a rash. Taking it with food helps reduce stomach issues. If you develop difficulty breathing, swelling of the face or throat, or a severe rash, seek emergency care immediately — that could indicate an allergic reaction.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Should I avoid any foods or drinks while taking this?

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: You should avoid alcohol as it can reduce the effectiveness and increase side effects. Also, some people find that dairy products can interfere with absorption, so try to take the capsule at least an hour before or after consuming dairy.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Good to know. How much does it cost with my insurance?

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: With your plan, the copay is $10. Would you like a bag?

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Yes, please. Thank you for explaining everything so clearly.

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: You're welcome. Feel free to call us if you have any questions once you start taking it.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: Good evening. What brings you to the emergency room tonight?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I was playing basketball and I fell hard on my wrist. It's really swollen and I can't move it without severe pain.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: I'm sorry to hear that. Let me take a look. When did this happen?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: About an hour ago. I put ice on it right away, but the swelling hasn't gone down.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: You did the right thing with the ice. Can you rate the pain from one to ten?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: It's about an eight when I try to move it, and maybe a five when I keep it still.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: I can see significant swelling and some bruising. We'll need an X-ray to determine if it's a fracture or a sprain. Are you allergic to any medications?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: No known allergies.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: Good. I'm going to give you some pain relief while you wait for the X-ray. Have you eaten anything in the last few hours?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I had lunch around noon, but nothing since then.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: I'll give you ibuprofen and apply a temporary splint to immobilize the wrist. The X-ray technician should be able to see you within thirty minutes.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Will I need a cast if it's broken?

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: That depends on the type of fracture. A simple fracture may just need a splint or a removable brace. A more complex break might require a cast for four to six weeks. The doctor will explain everything after reviewing the images.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: I really hope it's just a sprain. I have an important presentation at work next week.

lines.speaker: Nurse
lines.text: Let's not worry until we see the results. Even if it is a fracture, most wrist injuries heal well with proper treatment. I'll get you that pain medication now.

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: Good morning! I'm Dr. Park. Have a seat and make yourself comfortable. When was your last dental visit?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Good morning. I think it's been about eighteen months. I know that's longer than recommended.

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: Don't worry, you're here now. Are you experiencing any pain, sensitivity, or other dental issues?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Actually, yes. I've been having some sensitivity on my upper left side, especially when I drink cold water or eat ice cream.

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: That could be a few things — a cavity, gum recession, or worn enamel. I'll take a close look. First, let me do a full examination and we'll take some X-rays.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Sounds good. I'm also wondering about teeth whitening. Is that something you offer?

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: We do, but let's address any health issues first. Open wide, please.

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: I can see the issue. You have a small cavity on your upper left molar — that's what's causing the sensitivity. The good news is it's in the early stages, so a simple filling will take care of it.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Will the filling hurt? I have to admit I'm a bit anxious about dental procedures.

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: I completely understand. We'll use a local anesthetic so you won't feel any pain during the procedure. Most patients say they only feel a slight pressure. The whole thing takes about twenty minutes.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: That's not as bad as I imagined. How much will it cost?

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: With your insurance plan, a composite filling should be covered at 80%. Your out-of-pocket cost would be around $40 to $60. Shall we schedule it?

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Yes, let's do it. Can I get the filling done today?

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: We have an opening in about an hour if you can wait. In the meantime, I'd also recommend a professional cleaning — I can see some tartar buildup along your lower front teeth.

lines.speaker: Patient
lines.text: Let's do both today and get everything taken care of at once.

lines.speaker: Dentist
lines.text: Perfect. Going forward, I'd recommend coming in every six months for cleanings and using a sensitivity toothpaste daily. That will help protect against future cavities.
